payload.discussion: The principle suggests that robustness in deep sequence models should be addressed at the structural initialization phase rather than solely through loss functions or data augmentation. It implies a broader design rule: when a theoretical framework relies on idealized data conditions, embedding a probabilistic observation model into the foundation can preserve efficiency while enhancing reliability. The boundary condition is the availability of a tractable inference method (like the Kalman filter for linear systems) to compute the required posterior without prohibitive computational overhead.
payload.evidence: The work demonstrates that the standard HiPPO framework's assumption of noise-free data is 'often violated in practice,' causing performance drops. By shifting the mathematical formulation to treat data as 'noisy outputs of a latent system,' the derived initialization successfully 'improves the resistance of state-space models to noise both at training and inference time' as validated in experiments on LSSL.

concept_records.canonical_label: Initialization as Latent State Inference

payload.abstract_signature: Initializing dynamical systems by inferring the posterior of a latent state from noisy observations yields greater robustness than projecting observations directly as control inputs. This principle holds when the data generation process involves significant measurement noise that violates deterministic control assumptions.
payload.argument: Initializing dynamical systems by inferring the posterior of a latent state from noisy observations yields greater robustness than projecting observations directly as control inputs. This principle holds when the data generation process involves significant measurement noise that violates deterministic control assumptions.
payload.canonical_key: initializing dynamical systems by inferring posterior of latent state from noisy observations yields greater robustness than projecting observations directly as control inputs prin
payload.discussion: The principle suggests that for any sequence model relying on fixed spectral or polynomial initializations, incorporating a noise model into the derivation of those initial parameters is critical for real-world deployment. It implies that 'optimal' bases derived under idealized mathematical assumptions (like orthogonality over clean functions) may be suboptimal for practical tasks involving stochastic observations. The limitation lies in the complexity of deriving closed-form posteriors for non-linear or non-Gaussian systems, which may restrict immediate application to more complex architectures without approximation. Nevertheless, the boundary condition is clear: whenever the signal-to-noise ratio is low, initialization strategies must account for observation uncertainty to prevent error propagation through the recurrent dynamics.
payload.evidence: The work demonstrates that reformulating the HiPPO initialization from a noise-free control problem to a latent variable inference problem results in state space models that maintain performance on noisy sequences where the original HiPPO initialization degrades. The derivation explicitly shows that the optimal dynamics under noise differ from the noise-free optimal dynamics, and applying the noise-free solution to noisy data leads to suboptimal state tracking. Empirical results on the LSSL architecture confirm that the uncertainty-aware initialization preserves accuracy under varying noise levels during both training and inference, whereas the standard initialization fails to recover the underlying signal effectively.

concept_records.canonical_label: Tokenization-Induced Embedding Distortion Principle

payload.abstract_signature: If an input sequence contains delimiters that force non-standard sub-word segmentation, then the resulting token embeddings will deviate from the distribution learned during standard training, leading to reduced classification confidence for safety filters.
payload.argument: If an input sequence contains delimiters that force non-standard sub-word segmentation, then the resulting token embeddings will deviate from the distribution learned during standard training, leading to reduced classification confidence for safety filters.
payload.canonical_key: if input sequence contains delimiters force non standard sub word segmentation then resulting token embeddings will deviate from distribution learned during standard training leadi
payload.discussion: The principle establishes a causal link between low-level tokenization artifacts and high-level semantic failure in safety models. It suggests that the robustness of a Judge LLM is contingent not only on its training data but also on the stability of its tokenization boundary conditions. When an adversary introduces characters like emojis that act as aggressive delimiters, the model's internal representation shifts sufficiently to cross decision thresholds incorrectly. The implication for system design is that safety mechanisms cannot treat tokenization as a transparent preprocessing step. Instead, defenses must either normalize inputs to prevent anomalous splits or train models specifically on tokenization-perturbed data. Ignoring this layer of vulnerability leaves systems exposed to simple yet effective attacks that bypass semantic analysis by corrupting the foundational input representation.
payload.evidence: The paper reveals that Judge LLMs are vulnerable to token segmentation bias where delimiters alter the tokenization process, splitting words into smaller sub-tokens. This alteration changes the embeddings of the entire sequence, directly reducing detection accuracy and allowing harmful content to be misclassified as safe.

payload.abstract_signature: If a system requires prediction of future abnormal events, then the forecasting objective must be explicitly coupled with anomaly detection logic to learn the temporal precursors of failure rather than treating value prediction and anomaly classification as disjoint tasks.
payload.argument: If a system requires prediction of future abnormal events, then the forecasting objective must be explicitly coupled with anomaly detection logic to learn the temporal precursors of failure rather than treating value prediction and anomaly classification as disjoint tasks.
payload.canonical_key: if system requires prediction of future abnormal events then forecasting objective must be explicitly coupled with anomaly detection logic to learn temporal precursors of failure r
payload.discussion: Standard forecasting models optimize for minimizing error in value estimation, which often smooths over the sharp deviations characteristic of anomalies. Conversely, detection models focus on identifying deviations in past or current data. This principle argues that predicting the *time* of a future anomaly requires a hybrid objective where the model learns to associate specific trajectory patterns in the forecast window with the likelihood of an impending anomaly. This coupling ensures that the model attends to the subtle signals that precede a failure, which might be ignored by a pure value-prediction loss.
payload.evidence: The lay summary states that 'no method has addressed the challenge of predicting when anomalies will occur in the future' and the proposed solution is a 'unified model that combines time series forecasting with anomaly detection'.

payload.discussion: The empirical lesson indicates that safety filters are sensitive to low-level input representation changes that do not alter human-readable meaning. payload.core_idea: The framework integrates anomaly detection directly into the forecasting objective through an Anomaly-Aware Forecasting module. This joint optimization allows the model to simultaneously predict future values and identify the specific time points where deviations from normal behavior will occur.
payload.discussion: This unified design resolves the disconnect found in sequential pipelines where a detector might flag an issue too late for a forecaster to react, or where a forecaster smooths over critical anomalies. By optimizing for both tasks simultaneously, the model learns to anticipate the 'when' of a failure as an intrinsic part of predicting the 'what' of the future state. This is particularly effective for Anomaly Prediction, where the goal is precise timing rather than just binary classification of past data. The integration ensures that the model's attention mechanisms focus on the precursors to anomalies, improving the lead time and accuracy of predictions in real-world scenarios.

payload.idea_text: The framework integrates anomaly detection directly into the forecasting objective through an Anomaly-Aware Forecasting module. This joint optimization allows the model to simultaneously predict future values and identify the specific time points where deviations from normal behavior will occur.
payload.mechanism: The Anomaly-Aware Forecasting module functions by modifying the standard forecasting loss to incorporate anomaly timing as a primary prediction target. During the forward pass, the model processes the historical time series context alongside the synthetic anomaly prompts to generate future trajectories. The architecture is designed to output not only the predicted values for future time steps but also a probability distribution or indicator for anomaly occurrence at those specific steps. This dual-output structure forces the shared backbone to extract features that are informative for both reconstruction and classification of future states, ensuring that the forecasting capability is not degraded by the need to detect rare events.

payload.canonical_key: integrating autoregressive dependencies into diffusion models allows generation process to condition on previous frames enforcing temporal consistency in image sequences mechanism
payload.core_idea: Integrating autoregressive dependencies into diffusion models allows the generation process to condition on previous frames, enforcing temporal consistency in image sequences. This mechanism leverages learned inter-image relationships to guide the sampling trajectory toward physically plausible dynamic structures.
payload.discussion: The mechanism addresses a fundamental limitation of applying static generative priors to dynamic inverse problems, where standard models often hallucinate structures that are plausible in isolation but inconsistent over time. By explicitly encoding inter-image dependencies, the model shifts the inductive bias from purely spatial realism to spatiotemporal fidelity. This is particularly valuable in accelerated MRI, where undersampling artifacts can be misinterpreted as pathology if the model lacks context from adjacent time points. The mechanism demonstrates that autoregressive conditioning serves as a regularizer that stabilizes the inverse problem solution across the sequence.
payload.evidence: The text states that the AID model 'can robustly generate sequentially coherent image sequences' and 'outperform the standard diffusion model and reduce hallucinations, due to the learned inter-image dependencies,' confirming that the autoregressive integration of pre-existing information is the driving factor for improved temporal consistency.
payload.idea_text: Integrating autoregressive dependencies into diffusion models allows the generation process to condition on previous frames, enforcing temporal consistency in image sequences. This mechanism leverages learned inter-image relationships to guide the sampling trajectory toward physically plausible dynamic structures.
payload.mechanism: The method operates by modifying the diffusion sampling procedure to accept not only undersampled k-space measurements but also previously generated or observed frames as conditional inputs. During the reverse diffusion process, the network utilizes these autoregressive conditions to constrain the solution space, effectively reducing the entropy of possible outputs to those that align with the observed temporal dynamics. This creates a dependency chain where each frame's reconstruction is informed by the history of the sequence, preventing the model from generating static artifacts or temporally inconsistent features.

payload.core_idea: The method addresses data scarcity in anomaly prediction by generating diverse synthetic anomaly patterns through a learnable Anomaly Prompt Pool. This pool adapts to input signals to simulate rare failure modes, enabling the model to learn robust detection features without relying on extensive real-world anomaly examples.
payload.discussion: The mechanism fundamentally shifts the training paradigm from passive observation of rare events to active simulation of potential failures. By decoupling the anomaly pattern representation from specific historical timestamps, the model gains the flexibility to recognize structural deviations that precede failures across different domains. The signal-adaptive nature of the prompts ensures that the synthetic anomalies remain consistent with the underlying statistical properties of the normal data, preventing the model from learning artifacts that only exist in unrealistic synthetic scenarios. Consequently, the forecasting component becomes sensitive to subtle precursors that traditional methods might overlook due to a lack of positive training examples.

payload.mechanism: The mechanism operates by maintaining a repository of learnable vectors, termed the Anomaly Prompt Pool, which are optimized during training to represent distinct anomaly characteristics. When processing time series data, the system selects or synthesizes prompts from this pool that are adaptive to the current signal context. These synthetic prompts are then injected into the model's architecture, forcing the forecaster to process and predict trajectories that include these simulated abnormal events. This process ensures the model learns the temporal dependencies leading up to an anomaly, rather than just memorizing specific historical instances.

payload.canonical_key: criticality of model components can be quantified by measuring their local reconstruction errors on synthetic data bypassing need for ground truth labels or loss functions metric t
payload.core_idea: The criticality of model components can be quantified by measuring their local reconstruction errors on synthetic data, bypassing the need for ground-truth labels or loss functions. This metric, termed Subset Fidelity, leverages the theoretical bound that global error scales linearly with local reconstruction errors in Lipschitz-continuous networks.
payload.discussion: The mechanism fundamentally shifts model modification from a gradient-dependent optimization problem to a reconstruction-based selection problem. It is particularly valuable for open-weight models where training data and loss functions are inaccessible. The reliance on Lipschitz continuity ensures that the local measurements remain predictive of global performance, providing a theoretical guarantee often missing in heuristic pruning methods. This allows for efficient structured pruning and class-wise unlearning in resource-constrained environments.
payload.evidence: The text states that the work theoretically demonstrates global error is linearly bounded by local reconstruction errors for Lipschitz-continuous networks and utilizes this to propose ModHiFi, an algorithm requiring neither training data nor loss function access.
payload.idea_text: The criticality of model components can be quantified by measuring their local reconstruction errors on synthetic data, bypassing the need for ground-truth labels or loss functions. This metric, termed Subset Fidelity, leverages the theoretical bound that global error scales linearly with local reconstruction errors in Lipschitz-continuous networks.
payload.mechanism: The mechanism operates by generating synthetic data from the input distribution and passing it through the target model. For each candidate component subset, the algorithm measures the discrepancy between the original component's output and the output reconstructed by the subset. This discrepancy serves as the Subset Fidelity score. Components with low reconstruction errors (high fidelity) are retained for pruning tasks or targeted for removal in unlearning tasks, depending on the specific modification goal.

payload.discussion: The mechanism distinguishes itself from traditional jailbreaks by targeting the preprocessing and embedding stage rather than relying solely on semantic persuasion or role-playing scenarios. The dual nature of emojis serves as a critical component; they act as structural delimiters that disrupt token boundaries while simultaneously introducing semantic ambiguity that can soften the perceived tone of a message. This combination creates a robust attack vector that remains effective even against state-of-the-art moderation models trained to detect standard adversarial prompts.
payload.evidence: Experiments conducted on ten advanced moderation models demonstrated that the presence of emojis reduced the ability to flag unsafe messages by over 14%. payload.idea_text: Inserting emojis into text manipulates the tokenization process of Judge LLMs, causing words to split into unexpected sub-tokens that distort sequence embeddings. This distortion reduces the model's ability to accurately classify harmful content as unsafe, effectively bypassing safety filters.
payload.mechanism: The mechanism operates by exploiting the sub-word tokenization algorithms inherent to modern LLMs. When an emoji is inserted adjacent to or within a word, the tokenizer is forced to break the word into smaller, often semantically disconnected fragments rather than processing it as a cohesive unit. These fragmented tokens generate embeddings that differ substantially from the embeddings of the original whole words, propagating errors through the transformer layers and degrading the final classification confidence for safety violations.

payload.canonical_key: integration of autoregressive modeling into diffusion processes enables generation of temporally coherent image sequences by conditioning each frame on preceding frames thereby lev
payload.core_idea: The integration of autoregressive modeling into diffusion processes enables the generation of temporally coherent image sequences by conditioning each frame on preceding frames, thereby leveraging inter-image dependencies to regularize MRI reconstruction.
payload.discussion: The mechanism directly targets the failure mode of standard diffusion models in dynamic medical imaging, which often produce anatomically plausible but temporally inconsistent artifacts known as hallucinations. By enforcing sequential coherence through autoregression, the model utilizes temporal redundancy as a strong prior, which is particularly effective when spatial k-space data is heavily undersampled. The underlying logic suggests that for dynamic inverse problems, modeling the transition distribution between states yields more robust reconstructions than modeling the marginal distribution of individual states independently.
payload.evidence: The work explicitly states that the autoregressive image diffusion model is designed to sample the posterior for accelerated MRI reconstruction by incorporating undersampled k-space and pre-existing information. The text confirms that models trained on the fastMRI dataset demonstrate the ability to robustly generate sequentially coherent image sequences and outperform standard diffusion models by reducing hallucinations due to learned inter-image dependencies.
payload.idea_text: The integration of autoregressive modeling into diffusion processes enables the generation of temporally coherent image sequences by conditioning each frame on preceding frames, thereby leveraging inter-image dependencies to regularize MRI reconstruction.
payload.mechanism: The algorithm functions by constructing an autoregressive diffusion process where the generation of a current image frame is conditioned on the sequence of previously generated frames. During the reverse diffusion sampling phase, the model inputs include the specific undersampled k-space data for the current time point alongside the latent or pixel representations of prior frames. This dual conditioning mechanism ensures that the stochastic generation process adheres to the physical constraints of the acquired k-space data while maintaining temporal consistency derived from the learned dynamics of the image sequence.

work_version_records.abstract: Recently, forecasting future abnormal events has emerged as an important scenario to tackle realworld necessities. However, the solution of predicting specific future time points when anomalies will occur, known as Anomaly Prediction (AP), remains under-explored. Existing methods dealing with time series data fail in AP, focusing only on immediate anomalies or failing to provide precise predictions for future anomalies. To address AP, we propose a novel framework called Anomaly to Prompt (A2P), comprised of Anomaly-Aware Forecasting (AAF) and Synthetic Anomaly Prompting (SAP). To enable the forecasting model to forecast abnormal time points, we adopt a strategy to learn the relationships of anomalies. For the robust detection of anomalies, our proposed SAP introduces a learnable Anomaly Prompt Pool (APP) that simulates diverse anomaly patterns using signal-adaptive prompt. Comprehensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of A2P over state-of-the-art methods, showcasing its ability to predict future anomalies.

work_version_records.abstract: State space models are emerging as a dominant model class for sequence problems with many relying on the HiPPO framework to initialize their dynamics. However, HiPPO fundamentally assumes data to be noise-free; an assumption often violated in practice. We extend the HiPPO theory with measurement noise and derive an uncertainty-aware initialization for state space model dynamics. In our analysis, we interpret HiPPO as a linear stochastic control problem where the data enters as a noise-free control signal. We then reformulate the problem so that the data become noisy outputs of a latent system and arrive at an alternative dynamics initialization that infers the posterior of this latent system from the data without increasing runtime. Our experiments show that our initialization improves the resistance of state-space models to noise both at training and inference time.

work_version_records.abstract: We developed a Geneformer model with an expanded pretraining dataset of more than 100 million single-cell human transcriptomes. The increased data diversity and model size improved downstream biological predictions in network biology. We implemented model quantization, which retained the model’s biological knowledge while significantly reducing GPU compute requirements, thereby improving model accessibility.

payload.title: Emoji Attack: Enhancing Jailbreak Attacks Against Judge LLM Detection

payload.abstract: Jailbreaking techniques trick Large Language Models (LLMs) into producing restricted output, posing a potential threat. One line of defense is to use another LLM as a Judge to evaluate the harmfulness of generated text. However, we reveal that these Judge LLMs are vulnerable to token segmentation bias, an issue that arises when delimiters alter the tokenization process, splitting words into smaller sub-tokens. This alters the embeddings of the entire sequence, reducing detection accuracy and allowing harmful content to be misclassified as safe. In this paper, we introduce Emoji Attack, a novel strategy that amplifies existing jailbreak prompts by exploiting token segmentation bias. Our method leverages in-context learning to systematically insert emojis into text before it is evaluated by a Judge LLM, inducing embedding distortions that significantly lower the likelihood of detecting unsafe content. Unlike traditional delimiters, emojis also introduce semantic ambiguity, making them particularly effective in this attack. Through experiments on state-of-the-art Judge LLMs, we demonstrate that Emoji Attack substantially reduces the unsafe prediction rate, bypassing existing safeguards.

keywords: LLM safety; Jailbreaking Attacks; Judge LLMs; Token Segmentation
